Atty. MARK K. SLATER, Esq. of Slater Law Group APC (Tel: 415-294-7700) for plaintiffs;
Atty. ANNE E. WADDELL, Esq. of Dentons US LLP (Tel: 415-882-5000) for defendants New York Life Insurance Company and NYLife Securities, LLC;
Atty. KRISTIN N. BLAKE, Esq. of Klinedinst PC (Tel: 916-444-7573) for defendant Joyce Yoo.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFFS MAGDALENE LEE and ROCHELLE YEH's Motion For Leave To File An Amended Complaint.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiffs Magdalene Lee and Bernice Yeh's (collectively "Plaintiffs") motion for leave to file an amended complaint is denied.

In this case, approximately one month after Defendants New York Life Insurance Company and NYLIFE Securities, LLC (collectively, "Defendants") moved for summary judgment or in the alternative summary adjudication, Plaintiffs moved to amend the complaint. Defendants oppose this motion on several grounds, including that it is untimely given trial is set to begin in less than two months, and granting leave to amend would prejudice Defendants by precluding them from conducting discovery on the new causes of action. While there is a strong policy favoring amendment so that claims may be resolved on the merits (see, e.g. Nestle v. Santa Monica (1972) 6 Cal.3d 920, 939), such motions are directed to the sound discretion of the court. (C.C.P. Sec. 473(a)(1)). A Court may deny such a motion when the moving party has been dilatory and granting the request will prejudice the defendant. (See, e.g., Melican v. Regents of University of California (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 168, 176-77 [trial court within discretion to deny oral request to amend during MSJ hearing where no excuse for delay and amendment would be futile]; Huff v. Wilkins (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 732, 746 [trial court within discretion to deny ex parte application to amend complaint 3 days before hearing on motion for summary judgment, where there was no explanation for delay in request to amend, and amendment appeared to be both futile and unsupported by the evidence].) While it is unclear whether the new claim is futile, and the Court could envision measures to mitigate some of the prejudice visited by a further amendment, the record in this case supports Defendants claims that Plaintiffs failed to exercise diligence. "[A] trial court need not have infinite patience in dealing with an inept pleader", particularly when the circumstances, including the timing of the request, suggest a "cavalier attitude on plaintiff's part in prosecuting [the] action." (Del Mar Beach Club Owners Assn. v. Imperial Contracting Co. (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d 898, 908-09 [but holding that, under the circumstances, leave to amend should have been granted].) Indeed, although Plaintiffs contend they were unaware of these claims until Defendant Joyce Yoo testified in June 9, 2020, Plaintiffs have had copies of the Policies at issue since June 2018 and in March 2022 Plaintiffs gave contradicting testimony. (Waddell Decl., Ex. B at 92:7 - 93:8.) In light of the foregoing, the Court DENIES the Motion for Leave to File an Amended Complaint.

Order signed.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of The Complaint (1st Appear. With Court Rept Fee)
Off Calendar Jun-21-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MARK K. SLATER, Esq. of Slater Law Group, APC (Tel: 415-294-7700) for plaintiffs;
Atty. ANDREW S. AZARMI, Esq. of Dentons US LLP (Tel: 415-267-4000) for defendants New York Life Insurance Company and NYLife Securities, LLC;
Atty. KRISTIN N. BLAKE, Esq. of Klinedinst PC (Tel: 916-444-7573) for defendant Joyce Yoo.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT JOYCE YOO's Motion To Strike Portions Of The Complaint.

Matter argued and submitted.

The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Joyce Yoo's motion to strike is denied. The causes of action for elder abuse and concealment support the claim for punitive damages. Plaintiffs can recover treble damages under CC sec. 3345 based on the punitives. (See Ross v. Pioneer Life Ins. Co. (C.D. Cal. 2008) 545 F.Supp.2d 1061, 1064.) Even without the claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress, plaintiff can seek to recover general damages based on intentional fraud/concealment and financial elder abuse. (See Branch v. Homefed Bank (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 793, 799; W&I 15657.5(b); Bonfigli v. Strachan (2011) 192 Cal.App.4th 1302, 1316.) In any event, the court is giving plaintiff leave to plead more facts to support the intentional infliction of emotional distress claim.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)
